data.title: CSX Corporation: Q3 Earnings Results Confirm It's A Great Value Play
data.description:
My History Of Covering CSX

I called CSX Corporation (NASDAQ:CSX) stock a "great choice for value investors" back in November 2024, and rated it as a "Buy". I haven't updated my rating ever since. We all could have seen value stocks noticeably underperforming versus the growth stocks (in most cases), so I wasn't really surprised to see CSX trading at a total gain of 5.40% since November, while the S&P 500 index (SPY) gained a massive 12.08% for the same time.

Now, I'd like to update my take on CSX and see what has changed in the firm's corporate approach, in the overall profitability, and in the upcoming prospects. Probably, I should reassess my "Buy" on CSX after the firm's recently released Q3 2025 earnings results, who knows?

CSX's Q3 2025 Earnings Review

The company's revenues of $3.59 billion for Q3 were down slightly by 0.8% on a YoY basis, but they came in with a 0.48% beat to the initial consensus, and helped the adjusted EPS of $0.44 also beat the Street's estimates (by 3.73%). Those 44 cents per share were lower than last year's 46 cents, but they absorbed a meaningful non-cash goodwill impairment of ~$164 million related to Quality Carriers. Without that goodwill impact, CSX's adjusted EBIT would be pushed to ~$1.25 billion, according to the management's commentary, and as a result, the adjusted EPS would have been close to $0.51 if my math is correct.

Third quarter reported operating income was $1.1 billion and earnings per share was $0.37. These figures include $164 million and $0.07 per share from impairment of the remaining goodwill related to Quality carriers.

Source: CSX's Q2 2025 Earnings call trnascript

That goodwill may be a bit of a red flag, I agree, but it was likely a one-off cost, which is described as "discrete unfavorable impact" by the executives. Overall, I see that CSX's volumes are ticking up slightly (+1% YoY in Q3), but the mix turned out to be diversified enough to offset major weaknesses in the coal division, as well as in chemicals and agro products.

Also, what I liked to see was the continuation of unit economics strengthening. For example, the car miles per day increased to 136.6 in Q3, and it's a major efficiency gain compared to what we saw back in 2023 or 2024. In addition to that, fuel efficiency has also improved over the past few quarters:

After they started their intermodal service offerings into the Northeast region back in Q2 2026, CSX didn't stop in Q3 and basically expanded further on this front by focusing on the Howard Street Tunnel project, which has been finished ahead of schedule (just like the Blue Ridge subdivision project):

I want to highlight 2 major wins for us, the Howard Street Tunnel and the Blue Ridge subdivision projects. Both were extremely complex efforts and finished slightly ahead of schedule, a tremendous accomplishment, and I want to give a big thank you to everyone on our team who is involved to make that happen. With these projects complete, we now have full network access, positioning us for greater capacity and resiliency as we go forward. In closing, I couldn't be prouder of the team. The momentum this quarter is real, and we're going to keep it going. We're not done improving, innovating or delivering for our customers and shareholders and each other.

Source: CSX's Q2 2025 Earnings call trnascript

Zooming out for a bit, we see that CSX has been trying to adopt a more intermodal-driven and less coal-centric operating strategy for the past few years. As one of its recent strategies, CSX partnered with BNSF and announced a joint coast-to-coast service linking BNSF's west coast network with CSX's route to the Carolinas and Florida. The stock was down initially, but now it's rising, and it's about to open with a gain on the better-than-expected Q3 earnings print shortly.

Anyway, I think CSX should have better margins in the next years once the main capital spending is done, and the management starts to optimize the OPEX with a clearer focus, so to speak. The shareholder distributions through buybacks are likely to stay with us for longer, as the FCF generation capacity is going to strengthen after the rebuilds and expansions.

The number of shares outstanding has come down by 18.55% over the past 5 years, and even if we see some earnings deterioration lately, the management keeps executing well on the existing share repurchases program.

The consensus is anticipating a long-term sales growth CAGR of about 4%, so amid some expected efficiency gains in terms of OPEX and volumes, the operating leverage is likely to lead to mid-teens growth rates on the bottom line. The current EPS consensus prices in a 5-year CAGR of ~11.9%, according to Seeking Alpha.

So, the forward-looking sales per share ratio, with all other fundamental metrics divided by the ever-declining share count, is supporting CSX's rally continuation, in my opinion.

Speaking of CSX's valuation update, I'd note that if we annualize its YTD shareholder distribution (a sum of buybacks and dividends), we'd get a FCF yield of ~4%, which is not bad for the railroad industry with relatively stable cash flows. The "reasonableness" of the stock's valuation is confirmed by its about-average forward EV/EBITDA ratio of ~13.22x:

That's why I'm going to leave my "Buy" on CSX as is. I'd not anticipate a constant double-digit return from the stock every single year, but it's still a great value play from the old economy, which is performing well and has clear prospects of expanding beyond its habitual capacities and volumes in the next few years.

Risks To My Bullish Update

I may be wrong by calling CSX a great value play because its reasonable valuation at the moment can actually be trading at a premium to its peers, given the existing difference in terms of their debt-to-equity ratios and EBITDA margins:

If the market decides to punish CSX for its worse-than-average leverage and margins, the stock can adjust lower shortly, likely providing minimal (or even negative) total returns to its shareholders.

Also, the proposed consolidation in the railroad industry - the $85 billion merger proposal between Union Pacific (UNP) and Norfolk Southern (NSC) - is likely to pressure CSX, along with BNSF, and lead to a consideration of their own merger to maintain a competitive position in the industry. For now, it's not happening, and the fact that we don't hear anything about merger plans may suggest that there's a risk that CSX will try to go on its own and will start losing its market share at some point. In this case, all the bullish arguments from my end regarding CSX's operating leverage and potential EPS double-digit CAGR growth in the future will be off.

Final Words

The risks of buying and holding CSX are high, but I think that CSX still holds great value for its investors. It's reasonably valued, in my view, and with better cost control ahead, it can achieve and probably even beat next years' EPS consensus estimates. I liked the improvements we saw in its Q3 earnings results, so I leave my "Buy" in place.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: Intel's Q3 Catalyst: Doubling Down On Dominance (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
Updating My Bullish Stance Ahead Of Earnings

Since the date when I upgraded Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) stock to "Buy", it has managed to double in price, and while the broader market has only strengthened over the same period, the outperformance of INTC stock looks extremely massive to me (+92% vs. +13.32%).

The firm is going to report for its fiscal Q3 2025 earnings next Friday (10/23/2025), and based on what I see, I think INTC's bullish momentum is set to continue after a likely more positive commentary from the management team. The recent corporate developments at the company and in the industry in general are giving me hope for further strength, and that's why I decided to leave my "Buy" unchanged for now.

Previewing Intel's Q3 2025 Earnings

Last quarter, I was previewing Intel's Q2 with a core thesis that the results and the commentary they were supposed to present to the public would become a real game-changing event for the market. I was wrong about the "highly likely consensus beating" on the bottom line level at the time, but the Q2 revenues of $12.86 billion managed to surpass the high end of guidance, and what the updated management team said at the earnings call indeed inspired optimism, and INTC stock price recovered back to its multi-week resistance level:

The guidance sounded very bullish to me: Intel projected revenue between $12.6 and $13.6 billion (that's a midpoint of $13.1 billion, -1.3% YoY). That's below last year's figure, but last year Intel's customers weren't expected to hedge massively against the new tariffs, and the very fact that the YoY decline was guided at only about 1.3% speaks a lot about Intel's revenue generation resilience, in my opinion.

When we're talking about corporate turnarounds, I think we should keep in mind that an immediate recovery of net earnings is almost an impossible thing to achieve, as it usually takes up a lot of effort and time to return the past operating leverage in place and optimize expenses. So, that's why when the management guided for a breakeven on the bottom line for Q3, it was actually a big relief to the market because the company had been losing money for the past 6 consecutive quarters, not counting the losses per share in 2023:

The ramp of Lunar Lake and the early stages of Panther Lake's maturity should lead to only 36% in Q3 gross margin, but it looks like a short-term pain for Intel's likely volume recovery ahead.

With higher liquidity Intel got from the U.S. government (converting grants to an equity holding at ~$8.9 billion), and the $2 billion investment from SoftBank, they can further work on leveraging Intel 18A and attract external customers. For Intel 14A, they're trying to ensure that their capital is only invested with clear volume commitments, so I think it should de-risk their spending plan in comparison to what we saw in the past.

So we do need customers outside of our own products business to drive volume through that business to make sure that we get -- to get the right ROI. And we're just articulating that to the investors so they understand what we're trying to do. We're not going to put capital in place until we have firm commitments from customers that they're going to use 14A, and that we will get the requisite volume that drives a good return.

Source: From Deutsche Bank conference, emphasis added by the author

Generally speaking, the cost-cutting initiatives are in full swing, and I expect to see a massive earnings uptick after the Q3 breakeven is reached. Moreover, I think that Intel could be able to deliver a few positive cents per share in earnings, thus beating the current consensus estimates this time. The reason is that last year, the firm's total employee count was ~108,900, according to Macrotrends. So, that was a 12.74% decline from 2023, but in 2025, I think we should see a more massive layoff spree at 31%+ headcount reduction. And a lot has already been done last quarter (i.e., in Q2 2025):

During Q2, we completed the majority of the actions needed to achieve our year-end target of 75,000 employees. These were hard but necessary decisions, and we reduced management layers by approximately 50% in the process.

Source: INTC's Q2 Earnings call

My main core assumption for Q3 is that Intel's EPS will be positive, not 0, as it's currently expected, because the management's cost optimization plan has already been initiated and is likely running on schedule. If so, it's going to be a nice catalyst for INTC, especially if the top line comes in with a slight beat to the high end of the internal guidance. Wall Street isn't putting any premium on top of the midpoint figure of the updated guidance, thus increasing the odds for beating, like in Q2.

In addition to the possible beat, I think that Intel will be vocal on the new partnership with Nvidia (NVDA), according to which NVDA will integrate Intel x86 CPUs into its NVLink ecosystem for rack-scale AI supercomputers, this way becoming a major customer of Intel server CPUs.

The news broke last month, and I think it's not just Nvidia. When the main chip company in the world comes to INTC and basically saves it (or at least helps it to weather and emerge from its corporate stagnation), it suggests to me that other players will try to mimic this move. This is not due to solidarity, of course, but likely because they'll think that Nvidia wouldn't have approached its rival if the latter didn't possess something the former needed.

Intel's Valuation Ahead of Q3 Earnings

Some people usually get spooked when they see a firm's next-year P/E ratio of 200-300x, and in some cases, the fears are reasonable, indeed. But I think Intel's 311x forward price-to-earnings multiple should scare us because the multiple is projected to fall to ~58x in 2026, and then to ~33x by the end of FY2027, based on today's consensus estimates:

I don't think that it makes INTC cheap - it's just reasonably valued, as its EPS growth rate in 2 years (on a YoY basis) is projected to be significantly higher than those of its main peers:

And there's a lot of potential that can come from likely earnings revisions as the cost-cutting and wiser capital spending cuts take place and lead to a faster earnings recovery gain in 2026-2027.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

The main risk to my updated thesis is that I can overestimate the beating potential in Q3. Last quarter, I was off in my assumption about the EPS beating, and Intel couldn't reach my desired bottom line level. The Q3 print can be just like that again, and if there's no internal progress made for the quarter, or if the management fails to comment properly and impress the analysts, there will be a post-earnings sell-off. This risk is especially high given the recent run-up in the stock price.

Also, I may be wrong in the way I view INTC's valuation. The stock is priced at a higher implied P/E in 2 years compared to both AMD and NVDA, and while it's explained by the firm's much higher EPS growth rate for the corresponding period, the overvaluation itself might suggest that INTC is primed for a healthy correction.

The Bottom Line

Probably, I should have downgraded INTC after it had doubled since my upgrade in January 2025, but I think that, despite some clear correction odds ahead, the updated management team is doing everything right. The upcoming Q3 earnings release might ignite another leg in INTC's rally if a) the company beats today's quite conservative consensus estimates with no premium put on the guidance midpoints, and b) the executives will comment on the ongoing progress and impress the analysts, as was the case in Q2.

I'm reiterating my "Buy" on INTC stock today.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: AG Mortgage Investment Trust PFD Update: Too Risky For My Taste
data.description:
Introduction

Recently, I penned an article about the Notes available to investors issued by AG Mortgage Investment Trust, Inc. (NYSE:MITT), a mortgage REIT, or mREIT, company. This time, moving down the capital structure, the preferred stocks are covered, those being:

  • 8.25% Series A Cumulative Redeem Preferred Stock (MITT.PA).
  • 8.00% Series B Cumulative Redeem Preferred Stock (MITT.PB).
  • 8.00% Series C Fix/Float Cumul Redeem Prfd Stock (MITT.PC).

In my last review of these issues, only Series C got a Buy rating. Now I’m rating all three preferred stocks as Holds for most investors, a Sell for risk adverse investors. Investors might consider owning the Notes.

AG Mortgage Investment Trust review

mREITs are highly dependent on variables mostly out of their control, such as interest rate movements, the strength of the economy and housing market, and changes in government policy. This makes it even more important to examine the issuer in depth as part of their due diligence process. Seeking Alpha describes this mREIT as (edited):

AG Mortgage Investment Trust, Inc. operates as a residential mortgage real estate investment trust in the United States. Its investment portfolio comprises residential investments, such as non-agency loans, agency-eligible loans, home equity loans, re-and non-performing loans, and non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities, as well as commercial loans and commercial mortgage-backed securities. The company qualifies as a real estate investment trust for federal income tax purposes. It generally would not be subject to federal corporate income taxes if it distributes at least 90% of its taxable income to its stockholders. AG Mortgage Investment Trust, Inc. was incorporated in 2011 and is based in New York, New York. AG Mortgage Investment Trust, Inc. operates as a subsidiary of TPG Inc.

This is how the mREIT describes itself (edited):

We are a pure play residential mortgage REIT with a focus on investing in a diversified risk-adjusted portfolio of residential mortgage-related assets in the U.S. mortgage market. We primarily focus on acquiring and securitizing newly-originated residential mortgage loans within the Non-Agency segment of the housing market. We finance our acquired loans through various financing lines on a short-term basis and utilize TPG Angelo Gordon’s proprietary, best-in-class securitization platform to secure long-term, non-recourse, non-mark-to-market financing.

With MITT, investors have access to:

  • Investment opportunities in the Non-Agency residential mortgage loan market through Arc Home, our affiliated vertically integrated origination platform, and various partnerships with originators
  • Proprietary, best-in-class securitization platform
  • High-quality investment portfolio built with a credit-first mindset
  • Seasoned management and investment team with expertise in the mortgage and structured credit industry
  • Long-term market opportunity generating attractive risk-adjusted returns

Source: AG Mortgage Investment Trust.

First to be examined is the Income statement, which shows that while MITT is profitable, EPS varies widely across the quarters and some calendar years (2018, 2020, 2022) were very bad in terms of EPS.

I look at the Total Common Equity, or TCE, to determine what value is available to cover the preferred stocks should MITT ever file for bankruptcy. Here we see a TCE ratio of under 1.5X, which is on the very low side and doesn’t account for the $95MM in Notes that would be paid off first.

Taking a longer view, the TCE was close to $600MM before COVID, which dropped TCE to just $174MM. They did convince holders of about $50MM in their preferred stocks to convert their holdings into common shares. While that improved the TCE ratio, it increased the shares entitled to the common stock dividends.

Preferred stock review

Price stability is not a feature of this set of preferred stocks!

Factor A B C
Issued 7-27-12 9-20-12 9-17-24
Size (MM shares) 2.03 4.57 4.57
Coupon 8.25% 8.00% 11.29%*
Call date 8/3/17 9/27/17 9/17/24
Price $22.74 $21.41 $25.20
Yield 9.16% 9.37% 11.23%

* Floating-rate formula: 3-mo SOFR + .26161% + 6.476%.

Analysis

  • All issues are cumulative which came into play in 2020 when two payments were delayed.
  • All issues are not eligible for the 15% tax rate.
  • All issues are past their Call date.
  • All rank equally but below the two Notes.
  • C provides investors with a floating rate coupon with a combined 6.738% fixed component which is a very nice amount.
  • My rough estimate would have new C investors “being whole” even with the price above Par if Called now as almost 2/3rds of a dividend is included.
  • As long as SOFR stays above about 2.5%, C should get Called before A or B.

Conclusion

Based on the current yield, C is far above the other preferred stocks. If rates come down, there is the risk AG Mortgage will replace it with a lower coupon issue. I don’t think they have the funds nor the financial strength to Call it and not replace it. If investors are wanting a fixed-rate issue, I’d rather own either of the Notes as they yield more, come with Call protection, and rank higher in the capital structure in the case of bankruptcy. Both currently trade above Par, so their YTM is slightly less than its yield but still competitive with the fixed-rate preferreds yields.

The negative with the Notes are those yields end in 2029, when both mature. Due to the low coverage and better protection the Notes provide, I’m rating all three preferred stocks as Holds for most investors, a Sell for risk adverse investors.

Portfolio strategy

As mentioned at this article’s beginning, AG Mortgage provides investors with a choice between the preferred stocks reviewed here or the two Notes rated as Buys in a prior article. With Egan-Jones rating AG Mortgage as BBB- (apparently their go to rating), investors need to ask are they being paid for the risk of owning assets having the lowest possible investment-grade rating? The next chart shows current spreads are very tight between USTs and Baa bonds so overall the answer is they are not.

Besides the safer Notes, investors looking for 9+% yields should check out my recent review the Citigroup (C) preferred (article link) which is much safer from a default-risk prospective. Since there is “no free lunch,” this issue currently trades $5+ above its Par value.

data.title: Investment Grade Outlook: Insights For October
data.description:
By Matthew Brill, Head Of North America Investment Grade Credit | Todd Schomberg, Head Of Investment Grade Portfolio Management | Craig Altholz, Client Portfolio Manager

The Federal Reserve (Fed) cut interest rates, Treasury rates moved higher, and financial markets reacted in ways that seemed counterintuitive. Plus, demand for investment grade remained strong despite yields coming down. What does it mean for investment grade bonds and the economy? We tackle these topics and more in our Q&A on how we’re navigating the bond market in October.

Craig: The 25 basis points (bps) rate cut in September was largely as expected, yet Treasury rates moved higher across the yield curve. Why did rates respond this way?

Matt: Rates can be volatile whenever the Fed shifts policy. The market starts focusing on what’s next, both for the path of cuts as well as what the cuts mean for the growth of the economy and inflation expectations. It’s currently pricing about one less rate cut by the end of 2026 than what the expectations were before the September Fed meeting.1

Craig: Some Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) members and economists have made the case for continued or even faster cuts, while others have argued for a more patient approach. Assuming Chairman Jerome Powell will be replaced by someone more dovish when his term expires next May, is this what the market should be pricing?

Todd: Even if the next Fed Chair is inclined to take rates lower, to gain FOMC support, the argument for lower rates must still be justified by the economic situation. Also, the Fed controls the front end of the curve, but as you saw, even the 2-year yield rose after the Fed cut. The market is going to move the curve, and if the Fed is doing something that the market perceives will, for example, be inflationary, rates may react adversely.

Craig: Matt, you’ve generally been fairly optimistic on the US economy this year. Headlines and forecasts throughout the year, however, have been gloomy at times due to tariffs, consumer confidence, the labor market, and other concerns. Do you still think the economy is on a good footing?

Matt: We’ve seen upward revisions to gross domestic product (GDP), solid corporate earnings, and continued projections for large capital expenditures from the tech sector related to artificial intelligence (AI). Plus, companies have been rewarded in the stock market. While you may hear about headwinds for the economy, however, that’s not what’s reflected in asset prices. So, while I’m positive on the economy, I’m not suggesting loading up on risky assets.

Fortunately, high-grade bonds are well-positioned to perform in a range of economic outcomes. With valuations the way they are, we don’t see value in trying to pick up the last bit of incremental yield. For the reasons I just mentioned, we’re also not getting overly defensive. It’s also why we think the Fed can continue to cut even though the economy looks okay. Since rates are still restrictive, there are still risks that could knock the economy off course.

Craig: Financial markets reacted in ways that seemed counterintuitive in September. Are we back to the world of “good news is bad news” and the market is all about whether or not the Fed cuts?

Todd: That’s what it felt like in the short term. I don’t think the economy is desperate for Fed support. But markets may want cuts, especially when valuations are stretched. Still, I think good news is good news… unless it’s too good and vice versa. We don’t want to discount what the market is telling us, but I think some of those oscillations are mostly about uncertainty, which we’ve had plenty of this year. If those reactions get too far away from our view, then they’re potentially buying or selling opportunities for us.

Craig: Let’s talk about performance. The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index was up 1.09%2 over the past month, and the Bloomberg US Corporate Bond Index was up 1.50%.3 You’ve noted valuations are tight, so how were you able to navigate this market?

Matt: You mentioned earlier that rates backed up after the Fed cut, but they’re actually lower on the month, and spreads are also tighter. We’ve been slightly long on duration, particularly on the front end. We’ve also been overweight credit risk, given the positive views we’ve discussed. We’re really just staying the course on that view. Earning positive carry has been the key. At the same time, because of tight valuations, we’re running low tracking error — or active risk — relative to history.

Craig: Yields recently touched year-to-date lows of around 4.25% on the Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index and 4.65% on the Bloomberg Corporate Bond Index.4 We’ve talked all year about how the steady demand from yield-focused buyers has supported the investment grade market. Has that bid for bonds started to fade as yields have come down?

Todd: No, we’ve had more than $1 trillion of issuance year-to-date,5 and yet market demand continues to be very strong. Last month, we had one of the largest new issues of the year — $18 billion from one of the large tech companies.6 It was more than four times oversubscribed. New issue discounts are razor-thin and spreads remain tight — all signs that demand continues to outstrip supply. While we may be past peak yields in investment grade, yields are still attractive relative to history. There’s also a fear of missing out on yields now that the Fed is cutting, which tends to drive increased demand. You’ll probably continue to see that as the curve steepens and investors who’ve been parked in cash can’t get the yields they’ve become accustomed to without buying longer-dated bonds.

Footnotes

  1. Source: Bloomberg L.P., as of Oct. 5, 2025. There’s no guarantee that estimates/forecasts will come to pass.

  2. Source: Bloomberg L.P., as of Sept. 30, 2025. The Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of the US investment grade, fixed-rate bond market.

  3. Source: Bloomberg L.P., as of Sept. 30, 2025. The Bloomberg US Corporate Bond measures the investment grade, fixed-rate, taxable corporate bond market. It includes US dollar-denominated securities publicly issued by US and non-US industrial, utility, and financial issuers.

  4. Source: Bloomberg L.P., as of Sept. 30, 2025.

  5. Source: Bloomberg L.P., as of Sept. 30, 2025.

  6. Source: Bloomberg L.P., as of Sept. 30, 2025.

Important information

NA4886138

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

A credit rating is an assessment provided by a nationally recognized statistical rating organization (NRSRO) of the creditworthiness of an issuer with respect to debt obligations, including specific securities, money market instruments, or other debts.

Credit risk is the risk of default on a debt that may arise from a borrower or issuer of bonds failing to make required payments.

Credit spread is the difference in yield between bonds of similar maturity but with different credit quality.

Tracking error measures the divergence between the price behavior of a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark.

Dovish refers to an economic outlook that generally supports low interest rates as a means of encouraging growth within the economy.

Positive carry is a financial strategy where an investment generates enough income to cover its financing costs, resulting in a net profit from just holding the asset.

Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of the price (the value of principal) of a fixed income investment to a change in interest rates. Duration is expressed as a number of years.

Fixed income investments are subject to credit risk of the issuer and the effects of changing interest rates. Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa. An issuer may be unable to meet interest and/or principal payments, thereby causing its instruments to decrease in value and lowering the issuer’s credit rating.

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) is a committee of the Federal Reserve Board that meets regularly to set monetary policy, including the interest rates that are charged to banks.

The front end of the yield curve refers to bonds with shorter maturity dates.

A basis point is one-hundredth of a percentage point.

Gross domestic product (GDP) is a broad indicator of a region’s economic activity, measuring the monetary value of all the finished goods and services produced in that region over a specified time period.

High yield bonds, or junk bonds, involve a greater risk of default or price changes due to changes in the issuer’s credit quality. The values of junk bonds fluctuate more than those of high-quality bonds and can decline significantly over short time periods.

Inflation is the rate at which the general price level for goods and services is increasing.

Interest rate risk refers to the risk that bond prices generally fall as interest rates rise and vice versa.

Junk bonds involve a greater risk of default or price changes due to changes in the issuer’s credit quality. The values of junk bonds fluctuate more than those of high-quality bonds and can decline significantly over short time periods.

Monetary easing refers to the lowering of interest rates and deposit ratios by central banks.

Many products and services offered in technology-related industries are subject to rapid obsolescence, which may lower the value of the issuers.

Relative value refers to the value of one investment as compared to another.

Spread represents the difference between two values or asset returns.

Tracking error measures the divergence between the price behavior of a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark.

The yield curve plots interest rates at a set point of time for bonds of equal credit quality but differing maturity dates in order to project future interest rate changes and economic activity.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of Oct. 13, 2025. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties, and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

Investment grade outlook: Insights for October by Invesco US

data.title: TSMC Q3 Earnings: Still The Best AI Play At A Reasonable Price
data.description:
Since my last TSMC (NYSE:TSM) article in July, when I reiterated a Buy, the stock is up roughly 32% to about $302 per ADR, versus ~7% for the S&P 500. That move reflects the core of my argument then: the market was still underestimating how long and powerful the AI cycle could be for TSMC.

AI infrastructure capex looks structural rather than cyclical. Hyperscalers are still lifting budgets, and sovereign AI programs add a steady second wave of demand. Against that backdrop, TSMC’s latest quarter kept the trend intact. High-Performance Computing was expected to be strong, but management sounded even more confident about 2026 than three months ago. The real surprise came from Smartphones, which rebounded as inventories normalized and seasonality looked healthier.

Q3 earnings

Q3 revenue reached $33.1 billion, up 39.5% year over year and 10.1% sequentially. Gross margin was 59.5%, operating margin 50.6%, and non-GAAP EPS $17.44. Inventory days were 74, consistent with stable utilization and clean channels.

HPC remained the anchor, supported by ongoing AI demand, and management said AI demand is stronger than they expected. Smartphones rose 19% sequentially with normalized inventory. IoT and Automotive grew 20% and 18%, while DCE declined 20% with weaker end markets. Advanced nodes drove mix gains: the N3 ramp is on track, and N2 and A16 are scheduled for 2H26. These nodes are tightly coupled with HBM, which continues to keep packaging capacity tight across the industry.

Margins improved even with overseas costs. The CFO expects dilution from overseas fabs to narrow to 1–2% in 2025 from 2–3% this year. Even with that drag, Q3 gross margin beat the high end of guidance by about 200 bps. For Q4, guidance calls for 59–61% gross margin, a higher midpoint than Q3, helped by mix and scale. Channels remain balanced, with no prebuild and inventory at healthy levels.

Q4 revenue guidance is $32.2–33.4 billion, roughly 1% lower sequentially and 22% higher year over year, with operating margin of 49–51%. Advanced packaging is now about 10% of revenue, reflecting Foundry 2.0 integration between front end and back end.

My take

TSMC continues to show operating leverage on both revenue and margins as AI-driven HPC demand accelerates. The tone was more confident than last quarter, which matters because it signals visibility into 2026 that I don’t think is fully priced.

The Smartphone recovery deserves attention. After several quarters of correction, a 19% sequential rebound adds volume support while HPC mix carries profitability. That combination, plus improving yields and lower overseas dilution, helps explain why gross margins can rise again next quarter despite Arizona and Japan start-up costs.

Management also said N2’s structural profitability should exceed N3, a signal that the next node transition may avoid the margin dip seen in prior ramps. With capex growth likely trailing revenue growth in 2026, free cash flow leverage can extend beyond this cycle.

For me, the debate is shifting from whether the AI cycle holds to how long TSMC can compound within it.

Valuation

At roughly $300 per ADR, TSMC trades around 31x forward non-GAAP earnings, about a 22% premium to the sector. Given nearly 40% revenue growth today and guidance for further expansion next quarter, that premium looks earned. On a PEG below 0.5, the stock still looks undervalued relative to its growth.

I still think TSMC is one of the best ways to play AI without overpaying. Besides, alongside AMD, it’s the only name with a Strong Buy quant rating, while the other presented peers sit at Hold.

Metric / Factor TSM AVGO AMD NVDA INTC ARM MRVL
P/E Non-GAAP (FY1) 31.01 52.06 60.84 39.87 313.63 100.96 31.76
P/E Non-GAAP (FY2) 26.52 38.01 37.94 27.98 58.93 76.09 26.43
P/E Non-GAAP (FY3) 21.57 28.97 25.97 23.33 33.24 61.61 21.31
Valuation Grade D F C− F B+ D− F
Growth Grade A− A− A A F A A−

SA (10/16/25)

Risks

  • Geopolitics: China–Taiwan risk is a tail event that trumps any near-term model. Tougher U.S. tariffs could force faster offshore buildout, which is costly and dilutive.
  • Customer concentration: NVIDIA, Apple, and AMD drive a large share of revenue, so their capex cycles directly shape results.
  • Packaging bottlenecks: CoWoS and related capacity can cap near-term capture if inference demand accelerates faster than expansions.
  • FX risk: A stronger Taiwan dollar compresses reported margins and ADR translation.

Conclusion

I remain on Buy. Three pillars drive the call: AI orders are still building, mix is shifting to higher-value nodes and packaging, and margins are set to improve again next quarter despite overseas dilution.

At about $300 per ADR, multiple expansion looks limited, but earnings power can still carry returns. I’m comfortable owning it into 2026 while those catalysts line up.

data.title: Boeing: The Turnaround Is Finally Taking Flight
data.description:
Boeing (NYSE:BA) is working on a multi-year turnaround with a core focus on products to improve the balance sheet. Key elements of that turnaround include increasing production on the Boeing 737 MAX and Boeing 787 program as well as the certification of the Boeing 777X and the Boeing 737 MAX 7 and MAX 10. Since my last report, the stock has retreated slightly amid continuing concerns on trade targeting one of its key markets. In this report, I provide a detailed discussion the airplane orders and deliveries for Boeing in September as these are essentially the building blocks of Boeing’s turnaround.

Boeing Logs Big Airplane Order From Turkish Airlines

For this report, I'm using a tool developed by The Aerospace Forum to visualize airplane orders, deliveries, cancellations, and associated values.

In September, Boeing booked 96 orders compared to 26 orders in the prior month. Orders consisted of 32 single-aisle airplanes and 64 wide-body airplanes valued at $11.2 billion:

  • Norwegian Air Shuttle (OTCPK:NWARF) firmed options for 30 Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • Turkish Airlines ordered 35 Boeing 787-9s and 15 Boeing 787-10s.
  • One or more unidentified customers ordered two Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • Uzbekistan Airways ordered 14 Boeing 787-9s.

During the month, the following changes were made to the order book:

  • Enter Air cancelled an order for one Boeing 737 MAX.
  • Yangtze River Finance Leasing was identified as the customer for one Boeing 777F.
  • China Eastern Airlines was identified as the customer for one Boeing 787-9.
  • Hawaiian Airlines (ALK) converted orders for four Boeing 787-9s to four -10s.
  • BCA Customer Finance converted an order for one Boeing 787-9 to a Boeing 787-10 and Hawaiian Airlines was assigned as the customer.
  • Air Algerie was identified as the customer for eight Boeing 737 MAX airplanes.
  • TUI assumed delivery slots for three Boeing 737 MAX airplanes from BOC Aviation.
  • China Southern Airline was identified as the customer for one Boeing 737 MAX.
  • Copa Airlines (CPA) was identified as the customer for two Boeing 737 MAX aircraft.
  • Donghai Airlines was identified as the customer for one Boeing 737 MAX.
  • Shenzhen Airlines and Xiamen Airlines were identified as the customers for two Boeing 737 MAX airplanes each.

September did not contain any major surprises in terms of orders. The orders from Uzbekistan Airways, Turkish Airlines and Norwegian were already disclosed. The order inflow for Boeing in September reflected the company’s key programs for cash generation and signalled continuing momentum for the programs. We also saw that Chinese customers continue being identified. This is a standard practice for Chinese orders that are often booked as undisclosed customer orders and identified once deliveries occur.

Boeing booked 96 orders and one cancellation in September, bringing the net orders to 95 units valued at $11.2 billion. In the same month last year, Boeing booked 65 orders and no cancellations with a net order value of $4.5 billion. So, order value increased driven by a larger order quantity as well as the mix leaning more towards wide body airplanes. Year-to-date, Boeing booked 821 orders and 47 cancellations, bringing the net orders to 774 units valued at $83.7 billion. In the comparable period last year, Boeing booked 315 orders and 43 cancellations, bringing the net orders to 272, valued at $22.3 billion.

So, overall we see a significant increase in the order inflow while cancellations are only slightly higher than last year. ASC 606 adjustments increased by 47 units. This was driven by an increase of 49 units on the Boeing 737 program and a decrease of two units on the Boeing 787 program.

ASC 606 adjustments might be a vague term. Therefore, I have provided a description form prior analyses about what they are:

ASC 606 adjustments are corrections to the backlog for orders where a purchase contract exists, but additional criteria are not met. A decrease in ASC 606 adjustments can be achieved when the order, in fact, does get cancelled or the additional criteria are met.

The total tally of adjustments now stands at 592 which covers 9% of the backlog. It does not mean that 9% of the backlog is necessarily prone for cancellation, but for nine percent of the backlog additional criteria are not yet met.

Boeing Airplane Deliveries Remain High

In September, Boeing delivered 55 airplanes, consisting of 41 single-aisle airplanes and 14 wide-body airplanes valued at $4.1 billion:

  • Boeing delivered 41 737 airplanes, including 41 MAX airplanes and one -700 for defense purposes.
  • Boeing delivered one four Boeing 767 consisting of two Boeing 767-2C used as a baseline for the tanker airplane and two freighters.
  • Boeing delivered three 777F airplanes.
  • Boeing delivered a total of seven Boeing 787 airplanes, consisting of six -9 models and one -10 model.

Boeing’s deliveries dropped by two units sequentially. However, we do note that Boeing hit a production rate of 38 airplanes per month on the Boeing 737 MAX program earlier this year and since June, the jet maker has been averaging 55 airplane deliveries compared to 44 deliveries earlier. So, we see that deliveries have definitely stepped up. Boeing closed the shadow factory for the Boeing 737 MAX in August. So, by now there is little if anything left from the MAX inventory. There still are some Boeing 787s in inventory, but delivery increases are now leaning more on production rate increases. That is also why Boeing besides being stable on the current MAX production rate wants to hike production to 42 airplanes per month starting this month with another hike planned for Q1 or early Q2 next year. In some way, things are finally aligning for Boeing in a methodical way rather than pushing too hard.

In the same month last year, Boeing delivered 33 airplanes valued at $2.3 billion. Year-to-date, Boeing has delivered 440 airplanes valued at $33.2 billion compared to 291 deliveries valued at $21.1 billion a year ago. In all of 2024, Boeing delivered 348 airplanes and deliveries actually collapsed in Q4 2024 due to the strike at Boeing. So, Boeing is set to have a substantial increase in deliveries this year as Q4 will see significantly higher delivery volumes while it is already significantly ahead of last year’s full year delivery figures.

The book-to-bill ratio, an indicator of supply-demand health, was 1.7x in terms of orders and 2.7x in terms of value. The ratio for the month mostly reflects a low order inflow. Year-to-date, the book-to-bill ratio in units is 1.9x and 2.6x in terms of value essentially unchanged from last month’s year-to-date ratios. It is still the case that production volumes continue to lack normalized supply capability as jet makers are still rebuilding production rates following the pandemic and supply chain disruptions driven by critical forging capabilities that became unavailable following the sanctions against Russia.

Conclusion: Boeing Sees Consistently Higher Deliveries

Boeing’s path remains one that has some hurdles to overcome. Shadow factories are closed allowing Boeing to clear remaining inventories and direct resources to production increases. It will likely take another two years from now until Boeing is at production levels that it had initially targeted but with each rate we will see cash flows getting better. The key, however, is that all of it needs to happen in a setting with safety and quality as the cornerstone. Cash flows and value creation should be a result of the company’s quality objective rather than cash flows and value creation being a target and quality being the byproduct of that and I do believe that the current CEO is transforming the company accordingly. There remain significant hurdles such as the certification of the remaining MAX models and the Boeing 777X which could result in additional costs, but overall Boeing is in a much better position than a year or two ago when it comes to developing a sustainable growth path.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Praxis: Back From The Dead, Ulixacaltamide Returns In Essential Tremor With Caveats
data.description:
Praxis Precision Medicines Overview

The unpredictability of biotechnology investing never stops. Praxis Precision Medicines (NASDAQ:PRAX) is a prime example. I last covered the stock in March following a recommendation from the Independent Data Monitoring Committee to stop its essential tremor, or ET, study of ulixacaltamide, a T-type calcium channel blocker. Recall that ulixacaltamide missed its main goal in a Phase 2 trial (but informed the functional endpoint choice later), prompting the company to pursue functional endpoints in the later Phase 3 program (Essential3). So, I figured ulixacaltamide was out of the picture in ET, and I was actually more interested in its epilepsy assets - namely vormatrigine and relutrigine. Given its beaten-down valuation following ET disappointments and its opportunity in epilepsy, I upgraded my rating to Hold.

Yesterday, Praxis's stock rallied nearly 200% after reporting Essential3 data. I have carefully reviewed the press release and slides and invite you to do the same.

Despite positive topline data, the overall Essential3 package leaves me wanting more. In the article that follows, I propose that while ulixacaltamide could be a credible new oral option with functional benefit, it comes with material caveats that could cap persistence and market share.

Recent Developments

Let's begin by briefly reviewing what the Essential3 program was. It consisted of two studies. Study 1 was a parallel-group trial that measured the mADL11 mean at Week 8. Study 2 was a randomized withdrawal, responder-enriched trial that measured response maintenance.

mADL11 is a patient-reported functional scale that scores 11 items from the TETRAS-ADL (thinking, dressing, using keys, hygiene, writing, and so on). mADL11 was also the basis for responder definition in Study 2.

As you can see in Slide 13 above, ulixacaltamide handily bests placebo in mADL11 at Day 56.

In Study 2, 55% maintained a response on the drug vs. 33% on placebo. This was narrowly statistically significant (p=0.0369). While the first key secondary (rate of disease change) was significant, PGI-C/CGI-S were not.

While ulixacaltamide is described as "generally well tolerated," with no drug-related SAEs, TEAEs were common and drove a lot of discontinuations. Drug-related d/c was 27-28% vs. just ~2% for placebo. Frequent AEs (≥10%) included dizziness, brain fog, constipation, paraesthesia, insomnia, and euphoric mood.

Following Essential3 data, Praxis requested a pre-NDA meeting and is targeting an NDA for early 2026 pending FDA dialogue.

The question of the day is how a study that was advised to be stopped for futility ended up hitting on its primary endpoint. It is likely that the non-binding futility interim analysis underestimated the effect due to timing (Day 84), smaller N, early dropouts, and higher variance. The final, larger dataset, plus the Week 8 focus, reversed those optics. While Praxis amended the protocol (pre-unblinding) to move primary analysis from Day 84 to Day 56, Day 84 was still positive as well. Surely, the endpoint switch will be scrutinized, but it is unlikely to be a "show-stopper" given the pre-unblinded amendment, orthogonal randomized-withdrawal confirmation, Day-84 support, and strong missing-data sensitivity.

In my eyes, the biggest approval risk is tolerability (discontinuations) affecting overall risk-benefit and real-world adherence. ET is a chronic condition. Although there is just one FDA-approved medicine for ET (propranolol), many off-label generics are widely utilized to treat it as well (like primidone and topiramate). So, this is a bit of an entrenched market despite the need. For instance, in a recent study, 89% of ET patients received some Rx over two years. That being said, efficacy is mixed, and the evidence is sparse for any medicines outside of propranolol and primidone. Another approval risk is that the package leans heavily on mADL11 with limited objective tremor quantification (based on publicly available information).

Let's assume ulixacaltamide is approved with a clean label for ET. What will the payer playbook look like? I would expect step therapy (fail propranolol/primidone ± one off-label generic) before ulixacaltamide coverage. Neurologists will be the ones predominantly prescribing ulixacaltamide, and they are likely to trial ulixacaltamide after first-line/generics or in patients intolerant/inadequately controlled. I think it is likely that ulixacaltamide's tolerability issues (dizziness, brain fog) will cap on-drug persistence vs. generics. As a result, ulixacaltamide's most plausible near-term role is 2L/3L oral. I don't envision that ulixacaltamide will immediately displace cheap off-labels.

What is the precedent for breaking through off-label markets? It depends on the drug. VMAT2 inhibitors, like Neurocrine's (NBIX) Ingrezza, quickly displaced weak off-label options (benzos/anticholinergics) in tardive dyskinesia (another movement disorder). This was because VMAT2 inhibitors are clearly superior with manageable safety. A counterexample may be Nuedexta in PBA. Neudexta entered an off-label SSRI/TCA market, but its adoption was only moderate, given tolerability/interactions concerns and payer scrutiny.

Modeling Ulixacaltamide

While Praxis boasts "7 million" U.S. patients with ET, the addressable population is much lower (~1.2 million). A large portion of ET patients do not seek treatment, and the patients who do are often non-adherent to their medicine. Given the combination of ulixacaltamide tolerability concerns (which could actually be understated in a clinical trial setting), existing adherence issues in ET, and generically available drugs, my base market share penetration is 8%. It is reasonable to me that 1 in 2 patients could trial ulixacaltamide in the real world and decide not to persist. So, my "8%" estimate really reads like "16%, but with 50% stopping" within a year. Ordinarily, the probability of success for a pre-NDA drug would be >80%, but ulixacaltamide's tolerability and regulatory optics demand a bit more caution than average. My $6,000/year drug price is payer-adjusted (not WAC). I am assuming a good amount of GTN (40-50%) for ulixacaltamide given off-label anchors, rebates, and step edits. For context, new neuro orals like Xcopri (epilepsy) and Qulipta (migraine) have WACs near $14k/yr. COGS of 10% reflects oral small-molecule costs. 30% of OpEx involves SG&A and post-marketing R&D at the product level.

So, my current risk-adjusted NPV for ulixacaltamide in ET (U.S. market only) is nearly $1.25 billion. Should ulixacaltamide achieve approval with a clean label (increasing the "probability of success" to 100%), the rNPV jumps to $1.662 billion.

Today, Praxis's market capitalization is $3.65 billion. So, it appears to reasonably account for ulixacaltamide's potential in ET.

Of course, the usual modeling caveats apply. I am not omniscient and cannot see into the future. If some of these inputs are off by a wide margin, my final NPV could be materially wrong. These are assumptions, and they are subject to change. I've modeled drugs pre-approval before and have had to change input assumptions in the past! Modeling is never about being perfect. It's about giving your best attempt based on available information and adjusting accordingly as new information becomes available.

During its call, management focused on real-world AE management (e.g., counseling, hydration, dose flexibility post-approval) and floated a multi-billion-dollar sales potential. Moreover, Praxis has already agreed to a long-term safety bar (targeting ~1,000 total exposures), reducing regulatory risk.

Praxis Pipeline

Outside of ET, Praxis is making great progress with its epilepsy pipeline.

Both vormatrigine and relutrigine are in registration-enabling trials. For detailed information on these drugs, I'd recommend reading my last PRAX analysis. To briefly summarize the opportunities, while both antiepileptics are differentiated, vormatrigine faces a crowded field in FOS and generalized epilepsy. Simultaneously, relutrigine targets a very small market in DEEs. As I pointed out in March, should vormatrigine secure a niche and relutrigine add multiple DEE subtypes (e.g., Dravet, Lennox, SCN2A), both could be "blockbusters" in their own rights. However, the path forward is not a straight line.

PRAX Stock Financial Health

As of June 30, Praxis held "approximately $447 million" in cash and investments.

Following ET data, Praxis announced an intention to raise equity, as much as 15% of stock. Details are sparse at writing, but this could be in the "ballpark" of between $400 million and $500 million. Given that Praxis is nearing what figures to be a costly launch in ET, this is a wise decision.

Last quarter, the company "burned" (cash from operations) $54.7 million. Assuming a stable burn rate and ~$850 million in cash and investments when the latest offering closes, Praxis's cash runway extends into 2028. In other words, the equity raise should give Praxis enough money to launch in ET without limitations while supporting its R&D work in epilepsy.

Final Thoughts

While Praxis has pulled ulixacaltamide out from its apparent ET grave near Halloween, it will need some "makeup" to be marketable. While the drug delivers replicated functional benefit in ET with solid statistics and a reviewable endpoint change, the commercial onus will be on persistence. High AE-related discontinuations in trials and an off-label, heavy, step-therapy market argue for 2L/3L positioning rather than a quick blockbuster in ET. With PRAX at ~$3.65 billion, ET and epilepsy optionality looks reasonably embedded, meriting another Hold rating.

That being said, I can certainly see the appeal of PRAX in a barbell portfolio, and upside in ET could be tantalizing should real-world persistence prove better and/or pricing/coverage beat expectations.

Near-term catalysts for PRAX include pre-NDA feedback, long-term safety/exposure counts, and epilepsy read-throughs.

data.title: KBWB: Bank Run, As In Run From These Bank Stocks
data.description:
The Invesco KBW Bank Index ETF (NASDAQ:KBWB) has been representing a basket of a US bank stocks since 2011. That was after the 2008 global financial crisis. From the looks of it, this ETF is facing one of its most difficult phases in its 14-year tenure.

Because for the first time since the GFC, there are signs that banks are sitting ducks once again. Despite plenty of favorable regulatory adjustments, threats including a weaker consumer, bigger job losses, stubborn inflation, little help from yield curve structure, and rampant investor speculation are all starting to catch up with this industry.

To be clear, I have not been a big fan of the banking/brokerage industry historically. As I used to say back in my past investment advisory days, “I spent the first 12 years of my career working within big financial firms…and I spent the next 2 decades trying to shed light on what’s behind the curtain there.”

KBWB: Big, Strong, But Full Of Weakening Stocks

This nearly $6 billion in assets fund is squarely dedicated to owning bank stocks. However, with the definition of which companies are banks changing dramatically during the global financial crisis 17 years ago, the top names in the ETF more recently have been big brokerage firms. Those that at one time were investment banks and brokers, but today are full-service financial stocks.

KBWB tracks the KBW (Keefe Bruyette Woods Inc.) Nasdaq Bank Index. But do not expect this ETF to rely on bank stocks listed on the Nasdaq exchange. They can come from the NYSE as well and often do. The fund is big and plenty liquid for most retail investors, and its expense ratio is reasonable for an industry ETF, at 0.35%.

There’s also an above-average yield here, nearly twice that of the S&P 500. But in this modern market, an industry like this one can give back 2 years of dividend yield in a day. Or a couple of hours. Just check out that 52-week range. Mighty wide for a sector that used to be considered a “widows and orphans” part of the stock market. That was before the industry was “put in play” by turning non-banks into banks. Out of urgency at the time (2008).

The top 10 holdings are listed here. Those top 5 are the biggies, and they are followed by some of the major regional banks. In all these 10 make up more than 3/5 of the total assets of KBWB. There are about 20 more stocks that complete the picture.

Here’s a more detailed look. My highlighted points for this busy but informative graphic are just below.

Here’s what stands out to me:

  • The PE ratios are almost uniformly attractive if we compare these to the broad stock market. However, if the dung hits the proverbial fan, history tells us that KBWB’s holdings will be at the center of the market’s attention, not viewed as “cheap” stocks.
  • These stocks have done just fine. So this is not a “value” sector to me in that aspect of my analysis. It has been led by the biggest of these holdings, similar to what we’ve seen in the market as a whole the past 3 years.
  • In the last 5 years, which now excludes the 2020 pandemic crash, the median drawdown in this basket of stocks has been about 50%. The top 2 holdings, both Dow 30 stocks and larger S&P 500 components, have masked the risk underneath.

I’m a technician to the core, and when the editors asked me to check in on this ETF, I was glad to. Because I’m been mentioning my concerns about bank stocks from a technical standpoint to the investing group I lead for a while now. I see in this daily chart below an ETF whose price is hanging on the edge. That PPO in the lower half may be in the process of a major rejection of KBWB’s attempt to rally.

The fund has gone virtually nowhere net-net since the start of this year. So as we with many market areas I observe, the catch up trade has occurred. But now it is in peril.

The weekly chart below is of no help. Unless you are looking for a bearish setup. Look, my version of chart analysis is not foolproof. But when it comes to spotting major risk before it follows through, I’ve seen for decades that this type of picture more often means “look out below.”

But hey, maybe a social post will cause this sector to bounce. But I’m not going to consider that plan A here.

How To Make Money When Bank Stocks Fall

In fact, plan A for me could be to try to exploit a potential steep decline here. Without distracting from the main thesis here…that bank stocks look very weak to me…I’ll note that those who want to research and consider trying to capitalize on a decline in bank and financial stocks can look at tickers SKF, SKRE, and BKND. There might be others, and it is easy to look them up using Seeking Alpha’s investor tools.

There’s also the strategy of buying put options on a financial or bank ETF, though liquidity is an issue, as compared to doing so on a major broad market index ETF. But to finish where I started, banks are indicating to me that before too long, they could be at the center of investor angst, as the excesses of the past 15 years in markets and money finally start to crack.

And for those thinking “ah, he’s just a bear,” I’ll note that at all times, I play offense AND defense throughout my portfolio. That is, if the broad market flies higher (as it has been doing recently, and shortly after April’s Liberation Day) or plunges suddenly (as earlier this year and many times in the past), part of my portfolio is dedicated to being there to profit from that.

The market environment continues to be both the most opportunistic and most dangerous I’ve seen in 40 years of doing this. And as usual, if the banks hit the skids, that will be a red flag more broadly. The odds of that happening are increasing by the week.

data.title: VIGI: Far From Being The Best Option For Global Income Investors
data.description:
For investors with a large share of their portfolios invested in U.S. markets, which is almost everyone, investing in a global ex-U.S. ETF can prove to be a really good complement. There are many strategies to do, and today I will look at the Vanguard International Dividend Appreciation ETF (NASDAQ:VIGI), which is a large ETF in the space with AUM of $9.3 Bn, benefiting from the Vanguard name and its low-cost expense ratio. The goal of the fund is to invest in companies that have a history of raising dividends, with the idea that it will result in a resilient portfolio. In theory, this is a solid approach, but I will rate the fund a hold. I think that the quest for resilient companies comes at the detriment of total returns with a "high" measure of risk, while I previously covered the Franklin International Low Volatility High Dividend Index ETF (LVHI) with a strong buy rating in the past: a very strong fund with a similar approach but best-in-class returns and risks. In this analysis, I will compare the two funds to show you that VIGI is an inferior fund and that you should avoid it.

A Clash of Dividend Philosophies

While both ETFs are focused on dividends, they have very different approaches in doing so, and it results in two very different portfolios and risk/reward profiles.

The Vanguard International Dividend Appreciation ETF is aimed at dividend appreciation. The fund is tracking the S&P Global Ex-U.S. Dividend Growers Index and invests in companies that have a history of raising dividends for at least 7 or more years and excludes the top 25% highest yields as a way to ensure that the fund doesn't fall into yield traps. The aim is to buy mature companies with low-risk profiles and very stable distributions aimed at income investors, not necessarily high dividends. This is why the fund dividend yield is not very impressive, with only 1.91%. In addition, Vanguard's funds are known for their low expense ratios, and VIGI is not an exception. The expense ratio is 0.10%, not weighing on dividends too much and providing investors with a very cost-effective option.

With a very different approach but a similar goal, the Franklin International Low Volatility High Dividend Index ETF tracks the Franklin International Low Volatility High Dividend Hedged Index-NR. Explicit from the fund's title, the fund is aimed at income investors looking for a resilient investment, just like VIGI. To do so, the fund first screens the MSCI World ex US IMI (Local) Index-NR for the highest-yielding equities and then applies filters on volatility to keep only the safest companies. Strategically, I think that this is an approach that makes more sense than excluding the highest-yielding companies that could be quality ones with the proper filters, like VIGI is doing. The difference is clear: the dividend yield of LVHI is much higher at 5.04%. However, this is a more complicated strategy to implement, and this results in a higher expense ratio of 0.40%, which can be an issue for investors looking for low-cost ETFs only. I generally tend to agree, but in the case of LVHI, I really think that the cost is justified; you will see below why.

The direct conclusion that you can make from these first two paragraphs is that to be relevant, VIGI will have to prove to either be much less risky than LVHI or provide investors with higher total returns. If we turn to valuation metrics, it appears that it is more about the second option. VIGI's P/E ratio is 20.0 and its P/B ratio is 2.7, compared to 13.1 and 1.9 for LVHI. With these elements, you can conclude that VIGI is priced for reasonable growth, while LVHI is priced for distributions. If it were really the case, both funds would be interesting, but you will see below that it is not. On both risks and total returns, VIGI is doing worse.

Portfolio Construction: Two Very Different Exposures

From the part above, you already know that the two portfolios are going to be different, because LVHI basically invests in the companies that VIGI is excluding with its exclusion criterion of the highest 25% dividend yields. In this part we will look at sectoral and geographical splits to see the exact differences.

Sector

VIGI

LVHI

Financials

Real Estate

-

2.17%

As you can see, the portfolio of VIGI is clearly aimed at sectors benefiting from long-term growth prospects. The two funds have a very similar weighting to financials, but VIGI differs with its allocation to IT and health care. This is consistent with the fund's strategy, as mature companies operating in these sectors are often benefiting from strong pricing power and have the earnings needed to increase dividend distributions in the long term. However, in terms of risks, they also mean higher volatility, as they are sectors facing high multiples and sometimes difficult regulatory environments. I would call VIGI a growth-oriented fund, in opposition to the value profile of LVHI, as you saw earlier from the valuation metrics and as you see now from the heavier positions in energy and utilities and the fact that the fund is completely avoiding IT companies, which I find to be a good thing for an ex-U.S. investment used as a complement to, for example, an S&P 500 ETF that will be very heavy in tech.

Now, let's turn to the geographical split:

Country

LVHI %

5.40%

7.05%

Australia

1.70%

7.11%

India

4.70%

-

Italy

0.40%

6.62%

Other Countries

12.20%

15.45%

As you can see, VIGI is actually quite concentrated on key geographies, despite a high number of countries with very small weights. This means more risks, and not exactly what you would expect from a global ETF. VIGI has a significant part of its portfolio invested in Japan, nearly a third of the portfolio, and most of the 33.25% exposure in Asia of the fund. Japanese equity markets are known for high distribution companies but are suffering from low growth (but resilient) prospects. This significant allocation means that an investor of VIGI will be especially dependent on the evolutions of the Japanese market and elements such as economic health, corporate earnings cycles, and currency fluctuations. Any downturn in Japan would hurt the fund's returns, and the outlook for the country is not actually the most attractive. LVHI is better diversified, with a better spread across Canada, the United Kingdom, and Japan.

The higher concentration of VIGI can also be seen with the top 10 holdings:

The top 10 concentration of VIGI is high at 34.34%, more than a third of the portfolio, and significantly more than LVHI's 23.36% despite the fund holding more companies. The portfolio of VIGI is made up of 335 companies, close to twice the amount of holdings of LVHI: 173.

About the portfolio, a very differentiating element between the two funds is the portfolio turnover. The portfolio turnover of VIGI is only 18% against 93% for LVHI. This is consistent with the two funds' strategies: VIGI has a long-term dividend growth strategy, while LVHI is following the dynamics of the market with a more active approach to risk management (which is yielding more returns, as you will see below). A high portfolio turnover means higher costs, and this is one of the reasons behind the high expense ratio of LVHI, which will never compete with VIGI's low expense ratio.

The Decisive Factor

In this part, I will present you with the most decisive factors explaining why I think that VIGI is an inferior option: the fund provides investors with fewer returns and more risks—not a great couple.

Let's start with total returns:

The figures are very compelling about total returns: VIGI is underperforming on all time frames, without exceptions. Not a good start, but not enough to say that the fund is bad; we also have to consider the risks taken to achieve the higher performance.

About risks, let's start with the Sharpe ratio, measuring the return per unit of risk taken. Keep in mind that it is an incomplete measure based on daily volatility, which is why I will complete it with the maximum drawdown after.

VIGI's Sharpe ratios are 0.78 (3 years) and 0.37 (5 years), extremely low against LVHI's 1.89 (3 years) and 1.30 (5 years). This is of course due to the fund's lower performance, but also from the fact that its standard deviation is notably higher: 13.46% (3 years) and 14.55% (5 years) against 7.55% (3 years) and 9.58% (5 years). VIGI is thus a lot more volatile than LVHI on a daily basis, but I came across some funds with high volatility that were actually resilient when the market behaved badly. To assess this, we need the maximum drawdown.

Over the last 5 years, VIGI's maximum drawdown was -26.39% during the January 2021 market crash, while LVHI's maximum drawdown, which happened in the same period, was only -10.00%. If you are looking for a resilient income ETF to invest in global markets ex U.S., I think you should be convinced by now that VIGI is far from being the best option in the market.

Risks

The most evident risk with VIGI is the concentration risk, and on three different levels: geographic, sectoral, and on key holdings. I already discussed the Japan risk, so I will not elaborate further. Financials, information technology, and health care are together accounting for nearly 60% of the portfolio, meaning that the fund is heavily dependent on these three industries and facing risks such as sector-specific headwinds like regulatory shifts in sensitive sectors like banking or fintechs and the risk of a cyclical downturn in tech spending. The top 10 holdings are accounting for more than a third of the assets; this is partly offset by the fact that there is not one company overrepresented in the top 10, but it remains significant.

Currency risk should be considered as the fund is global but pays investors in dollars. Currently, as the dollar is weakening, this kind of exposure is good, but if the dollar were to strengthen, it would hurt the fund's returns.

Macroeconomic risks are to be considered, especially because of the geographical concentration and "relatively high" P/E ratios. The fund is exposed to the risk of shock induced by a global recession, currency spikes, rate hikes, or European or Asian shocks. If the current global central bank tightening cycle were to lead to an extreme downturn, payout ratios would be squeezed, and the fund's returns would be hurt. Of course this is an extreme case scenario, and if it were to happen, I would still expect the companies of the fund to suffer less than the broad equity market.

Conclusion

The Vanguard International Dividend Appreciation ETF is not a bad ETF, and it could be a good low-cost opportunity to get exposure to international dividend growers if it were including quality filters beyond the overly simple top 25% exclusion, which, quite frankly, seems like a way to avoid trouble without doing the necessary work to filter good companies. I rate the fund a hold because it will still get you returns on global markets and is a very cheap option, but the fund has not convinced me to be a good option for conservative income investors. I rather recommend LVHI for this type of investor, but you have to know that LVHI is currency-hedged, meaning that you will not have exposure to foreign currencies, which is a feature that you might look for.

data.title: Sell Gladstone: Price Decline And Dividend Cut Signal A Dismal Future
data.description:
Introduction

Gladstone Commercial is a real estate investment trust [REIT]. They acquire and operate industrial and office properties in the US.

Some Historical Measures

Gladstone Commercial Corporation (NASDAQ:GOOD) yields 10.49% as of the October 14, 2025, market close. High yields attract attention and motivate investors to initiate and/or even increase their investment in a security.

I do not believe that Gladstone will recover in terms of either dividend or price in the near-term. However, to the extent that this represents a “loser” stock in a taxable portfolio, the stock price-per-share [PPS] may enjoy some PPS appreciation by way of the Tax Loss Selling or Harvesting, Santa Claus Rally and the January Effect seasonal sequence that is about to begin.

If you believe that GOOD will participate in this seasonal pattern, you may decide to initiate a position in November. This may prove to be a good entry point for the long-term investor. However, I do not expect any PPS recovery to “stick” beyond January or February 2026.

Below is the chart of the stock price and dividend history and the dividend history for Gladstone. Dividends peaked and/or were at their highest level in April 2020 [see the green box and arrow].

GOOD has paid dividends of $8.655 over the course of its history. With the exception of the very first dividend paid in July 2013 [not marked, but visible on the far lower left in the above chart], GOOD is, now, paying the lowest dividend in its history, beginning with April 2025 [see the red box and arrow in the above chart]…and it just cut the dividend again, as follows:

The PPS decline and dividend cuts persist, despite excellent occupancy rates, at 98.7%, as follows:

GOOD beat on the top line, with a revenue surprise at $1.22 million above estimates, yet they missed on GAAP EPS, as follows:

Effectively, both Seeking Alpha and Wall Street analysts are recommending that you Hold GOOD, as follows:

There is not a large amount of analyst coverage.

Note that analyst coverage is higher for four of the five peers identified on the Seeking Alpha website [see below]. I would prefer to see more analyst interest in a security I am considering investing in.

Operating cash flows are not increasing in recent years, as follows:

Free cash flow per share is the lowest it has been for a decade, as follows:

None of these historical measures look good, as follows:

Revenue per share is historically low, EPS was favorable for 2024, but negative for the remainder of the past decade, share count has nearly doubled over the past decade, dividends per share, as noted, have declined, and the payout ratio is very, very high [see the above table].

I understand that many prefer to focus on Funds From Operations [FFO] for REITs. It complements other measures, like EPS. This is a non-GAAP measure, and I suppose, at the age of 68, I am a bit “old school” and take, with a grain of salt, a measure pioneered by the National Association of Real Estate Investment Trusts [NAREIT]. This is just my humble opinion.

As Pacifica Yield, a Seeking Alpha analyst, pointed out:

The REIT has dramatically shifted its portfolio mix towards industrial properties and away from office properties.

I think the above suggests that management has determined that what they have been doing in the past has not worked, and they are in the infancy of a significant portfolio shift. I would prefer a “wait and see” approach to watch just how management executes in the future, as they have not performed favorably in the past.

Summary

I am recommending that you sell GOOD if you own it, perhaps after the January Effect seasonal has passed, if GOOD appears to be enjoying the upcoming seasonal sequence, as addressed in the introduction of this article. If you do not own GOOD, I cannot recommend a Buy or Buy and Hold at this time.

None of the fundamentals impress me, and GOOD may be good in a year or so, but not yet. Management needs to successfully execute their new strategy as they significantly and “dramatically” shift their portfolio mix.

My Track Record…So Far

I wanted to take a bit of time to review my performance since I resumed article submissions to Seeking Alpha in July. Below is a summary of the first ten articles submitted over the past four months:

I summarized the above and provide results, below, where the data was captured on October 8, 2025:

I have had seven winners [green, in the above table] and three losers [orange, in the above table]. My biggest loser was CBZ. My biggest winner was SES [pleased that I recommended a STRONG Buy on that one]. My equal-weighted average is about 13.91%. The favorable results were driven by the SES Strong Buy recommendation.

I have had six sell recommendations and only four buy recommendations. I would prefer to focus more on the buy recommendations, but I search Seeking Alpha to identify extremes and cases where I believe I can be reasonably confident in my recommendation, and more sells than buys have presented themselves.

I hope to improve my results in future articles. My results and any results that you experience and are based on my recommendations are important to me. I want you to make money.

Thank you for reading my articles. I wish you the best in your health and investments.
